coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots with concentration-modulated fluorescence: Aggregation-induced multicolor emission
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Ya
Xie, Zhigang
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with concentration-modulated luminescence were obtained by pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and thiourea (TU). CDs exhibit multi-colored emission by varying their concentrations. As a contrast, Zr-doped CDs (ZrCDs) were prepared from CA, TU and ZrCl4 in a similar way. To our surprise, no concentration-dependent emission were observed in ZrCDs. Structural and optical characterizations prove that the concentration-tunable fluorescence is attributed to the aggregation between CDs. As the concentration increases, the aggregation of CDs results in a gradual decrease in the band gaps (from 2.18 eV to 1.56 eV), which ultimately causes the fluorescence to redshift continuously. However, ZrCDs are always in a highly dispersed state no matter how the concentration goes up, the zirconium complexes on the surface of ZrCDs prevents them from aggregation and maintains the band gap of ZrCDs at about 2.21 eV. Therefore, we proposed an aggregation-induced multicolor emission mechanism for CDs, which has a certain reference significance for exploiting the potential applications of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Zirconium complexes
Aggregation
Concentration-tunable fluorescence
Multicolor emission

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The mechanism of concentration-regulated luminescence of CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Excitation-emission matrix of CD1-CD6 in aqueous solutions. (b) Excitation-emission matrix of ZrCD1-ZrCD6 in aqueous solutions.

(a) The relationship between maximum emission wavelength and concentration of ZrCDs and CDs. The error bars are based on the standard errors of the mean of three repeats. Absorption spectra of (b) CD1 to CD6 and (c) ZrCD1 to ZrCD6. Optical images of CD1 to CD6 under (d) daylight, 365 nm and 500 nm irradiation, respectively. Optical images of ZrCD1 to ZrCD6 under (e) daylight, 365 nm and 500 nm irradiation, respectively.

(a) Full XPS survey and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, d) O 1s and (e) S 2p of CDs. f) XPS patterns of ZrCDs. (g-k) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O1s, S 2p and Zr 3d of ZrCDs, respectively.
TEM images of (a) CDs (CD1, CD2, CD3 and CD4) and (b) ZrCDs (ZrCD1, ZrCD2, ZrCD3 and ZrCD4), scale bars in TEM images are 200 nm. Inset: size distribution.

The fluorescence quantum yields of (a) CD1-CD6 and (b) ZrCD1-ZrCD6; PL spectra of (c) CD1-CD6 (the excitation wavelengths are 380 nm, 380 nm, 380 nm, 480 nm, 480 nm and 500 nm, respectively) and (d) ZrCD1-ZrCD6 (the excitation wavelengths are 360 nm, 360 nm, 360 nm, 360 nm, 380 nm and 380 nm, respectively) during quantum yield measurement.

(a) Zeta potentials of different concentrations of CDs and ZrCDs. Emission spectra of (b) CDs and (c) ZrCDs in solid state. (d) Optical images of CDs and (e) ZrCDs in solid state under 365 nm and 500 nm irradiation. (f) Absorption spectra of CDs and ZrCDs in solid state.
Plots of (αhv)1/2 versus hv of (a) CD1-CD6 and (b) ZrCD1-ZrCD6. (c) The possible spatial structures of CDs and ZrCDs.
Schematic illustration the synthesis of CDs and their concentration-dependent luminescence mechanism.
Schematic illustration for the varying band gap transitions of CDs and ZrCDs at low and high concentrations.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots with concentration-modulated fluorescence: Aggregation-induced multicolor emission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with concentration-modulated luminescence were obtained by pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and thiourea (TU). CDs exhibit multi-colored emission by varying their concentrations. As a contrast, Zr-doped CDs (ZrCDs) were prepared from CA, TU and ZrCl4 in a similar way. To our surprise, no concentration-dependent emission were observed in ZrCDs. Structural and optical characterizations prove that the concentration-tunable fluorescence is attributed to the aggregation between CDs. As the concentration increases, the aggregation of CDs results in a gradual decrease in the band gaps (from 2.18 eV to 1.56 eV), which ultimately causes the fluorescence to redshift continuously. However, ZrCDs are always in a highly dispersed state no matter how the concentration goes up, the zirconium complexes on the surface of ZrCDs prevents them from aggregation and maintains the band gap of ZrCDs at about 2.21 eV. Therefore, we proposed an aggregation-induced multicolor emission mechanism for CDs, which has a certain reference significance for exploiting the potential applications of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely explored due to their superior optical properties, ultra-small size, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity [1–8]. However, most of CDs have blue or green emissions, which severely limits their widespread application. Thus, developing full-color emissive CDs is considerably interesting and highly desired [9–14]. Usually, there are two strategies to prepare multicolor fluorescent CDs: (1) Modulating the fluorescence of CDs by way of changing solvents, carbon sources, passivating agents, temperature and reaction time. For example, Cai et al. synthesized blue, green and red emissive CDs from p-phenylenediamine and different modifying agents [15]. Lin et al. synthesized CDs with multiple-mode emissions by varying precursors and solvents [16]; (2) Tuning the optical behaviors of CDs via post-modification. For instance, our group developed a simple strategy to tune the photoluminescence of CDs by conjugation with vitamin C and acetaldehyde and successfully obtained green and red fluorescent CDs [17].

Recently, several groups [18–22] have discovered an exciting fact that tuning the fluorescence across the entire visible spectrum can be readily achieved by controlling the concentrations of CDs. Shao et al. prepared polymer CDs (PCDs) with multi-color luminescence by modulating the concentration of the PCDs [23]. Zhang et al. reported that CDs obtained from citric acid, l-cysteine and KCl exhibit concentration-tunable luminescence [24]. Chen et al. tuned the emission wavelength of CDs from 514 nm to 585 nm through increasing CDs concentrations [25]. Nevertheless, the mechanisms of concentration-adjustable fluorescence of CDs are not intensely investigated and clearly illustrated [26–31]. Therefore, systematic study on modulating the optical properties of CDs by varying concentrations is of great concern for revealing their fluorescence mechanism and expanding the practical applications of CDs.

Motivated by all the aforementioned issues, in the present work, we synthesized CDs with unique excitation and concentration-dependent luminescence via facile one-pot pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and thiourea (TU) (Scheme 1 ). The emission of CDs shows a red shift by 154 nm by adjusting the concentration of CDs. In order to investigate the origin of multi-emissions in CDs, we prepared Zr-doped CDs (ZrCDs) in a similar way as a control. Surprisingly,no multicolored emissions in ZrCDs were observed, even the concentration was increased from 0.002 mg mL−1 to 1 mg mL−1, meaning that zirconium complexes on the surface of ZrCDs suppresses the concentration-dependent luminescence of CDs. The morphologies and sizes of CDs and ZrCDs determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) demonstrate that CDs aggregate gradually with increasing the concentration of CDs, while, ZrCDs do not. The zeta-potential of CDs at low concentration (0.002 mg mL−1) is −10.59 mV, as the concentration went up, it changed to nearly 0 mV, implying that CDs at high concentrations are unstable and tend to aggregate into larger particles. On the contrary, the zeta-potential of ZrCDs gradually decreased from −4.75 mV to −17.83 mV with the concentration increased, indicating that ZrCDs are in a highlydispersed state. The band gaps of CDs deduced from plots of (αhv)1/2 versus hv lower from 2.18 eV (0.002 mg mL−1) to 1.56 eV (1.0 mg mL−1), the interactions between closely adjacent CDs become stronger as the concentration increases, resulting in a decrease in the energy gap. However, the existence of zirconium complexes prevents ZrCDs from aggregation and maintains the band gap of ZrCDs at about 2.21 eV. Based on the experimental results, we propose that aggregation favors energy transfer between CDs, decreases band gaps and induces the multicolor emission of CDs.
Citric acid (≥99.5%), thiourea (99%) and ZrCl4 (≥99.9%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Corporation. The ultrapure water was prepared from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA).

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained by using a Shimadzu UV-2450 PC UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence intensity tests were performed on a PerkinElmer LS-55 spectrofluorophotometer. The Zeta potentials of the CDs and ZrCDs were determined by a Malvern Zeta-sizer Nano. The morphology of the CDs and ZrCDs was measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) performed on a JEOL JEM-1011 electron microscope operating at an acceleration voltage of 100 kV. Absolute fluorescence quantum yield was calculated by an absolute quantum efficiency tester (Japan Waterfront C 9920-2). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of CDs were recorded on a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer from 4000 to 500 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 Multitechnique Surface Analysis.

CDs were prepared by pyrolysis. First, citric acid (CA) and thiourea (TU) (molar ratio 1:3) were dissolved in 5 mL of water. The aqueous solution of CA/TU was sonicated for 10 min, then transferred to a round bottom flask and heated to 180 °C in an oil bath for 1 h. After the reaction was over, the obtained dark brown solid was cooled to room temperature. The crude product was dissolved in water and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. The resulting supernatant was placed in a dialysis bag (Mw cutoff: 3.5 KDa) and dialyzed against water for 24 h to remove small molecules. Finally, CDs were freeze-dried to a dark brown power.

CA, TU and ZrCl4 (molar ratio 3:9:1) were dissolved in 5 mL of water. After ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, the mixed solution of CA/TU/ZrCl4 was transferred to a round bottom flask and heated for 1 h in an oil bath at 180 °C. After the reaction, products cooled to room temperature are dissolved in deionized water and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. Next, the supernatant was dialyzed in deionized water (Mw cutoff: 3.5 KDa) and dialysis for 24 h. Finally, ZrCDs were obtained by freeze-drying.

CDs were prepared by pyrolysis of CA and TU as described in the Experimental Section. Different reaction time and CA/TU molar ratios were screenedtooptimizethereactionconditions. When the molar ratio of CA to TU was 1:3, the reaction time was varied from 0.5 h to 1 h and 2 h, respectively. The obtained CDs was characterized by photoluminescence (PL) spectra and shown in Figs. S1–S3. The fluorescence intensity of CDs (1 mg mL−1) prepared at 1 h is the highest at the same condition. So 1 h was viewed as the optimum reaction time. The fluorescence intensity of CDs prepared from CA and TU (molar ratio 3:1 or 1:1) is much lower (Figs. S4 and S5) than that synthesized from CA/TU (1:3 M ratio). Moreover, although all of the CDs prepared under these five reaction conditions possess concentration-dependent luminescence behaviors, the red-shift of the maximum emission wavelength of CDs synthesized from CA and TU (molar ratio = 1:3, 1 h) is the largest (Fig. S6). Therefore, it is concluded that the optimum reaction condition is that the reaction time is 1 h and the molar ratio of CA to TU is 1:3. Fig. 1 a shows the excitation-emission matrix of CDs at different concentrations: 0.002 mg mL−1 (CD1), 0.05 mg mL−1 (CD2), 0.1 mg mL−1 (CD3), and 0.25 mg mL−1 (CD4), 0.5 mg mL−1 (CD5) and 1 mg mL−1 (CD6). CDs at any concentration exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence and their maximum emission wavelength redshifts continuously withincreasingexcitationwavelength. Moreover, as the concentration increases, the maximum emission wavelength of CDs is red-shifted by 154 nm from 461 nm to 615 nm (Fig. 2 a), indicating that CDs have significant concentration-modulated luminescence.

In order to investigate the origin of the concentration-dependent fluorescence, we synthesized Zr-doped CDs (ZrCDs) under the same reaction condition with CDs except that the molar ratio of CA, TU and ZrCl4 is 3:9:1. By contrast, different concentrations of ZrCDs: 0.002 mg mL−1 (ZrCD1), 0.05 mg mL−1 (ZrCD2), 0.1 mg mL−1 (ZrCD3), 0.25 mg mL−1 (ZrCD4), 0.5 mg mL−1 (ZrCD5) and 1 mg mL−1 (ZrCD6) have only a slight excitation-dependent luminescence (Fig. 1b) and their maximum luminescence wavelength was maintained in the range of 450–460 nm. Furthermore, no concentration-modulated red-shift luminescence was observed. The relationships between the maximum emission wavelength and concentration of CDs and ZrCDs are shown in Fig. 2a, respectively, which suggest that CDs have significant concentration-tunable luminescence, while ZrCDs do not. From the absorption spectra (Fig. 2b), as the concentration of CDs increases from 0.002 to 1.0 mg mL−1, the absorption spectra gradually expand, and the typical absorption peaks undergo red-shift. At the same time, the absorption intensity increases significantly. In contrast, although the absorption intensity of ZrCDs increases slowly with the increase of concentration (Fig. 2c), the absorption bands do not shift. By observing the images of CD1 to CD6 under the daylight, it can be seen from Fig. 2d that the color of the CDs aqueous solution is significantly deepened as the concentration of CDs increases. In addition, under the excitation of 365 nm, the fluorescence intensity gradually increases from CD2 to CD6 except CD1, because its concentration is too low and no obvious fluorescence is observed. Analogously, under the 500 nm excitation, the red fluorescence intensity of CD2 to CD6 gradually increases. On the contrary, the image of ZrCDs aqueous solution in daylight (Fig. 2e) is only slightly deepened with increasing concentration, and the blue fluorescence is gradually enhanced under 365 UV irradiation. Very faint red fluorescence is detected under blue light conditions (500 nm), even the concentration of ZrCDs is as high as 1.0 mg mL−1.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted to investigate the surface states of CDs and ZrCDs [13,32–34]. Fig. 3 a-e shows the XPS spectra of CDs. The carbon (C1s, 39.9 wt%), nitrogen (N1s, 19.1 wt%), oxygen (O1s, 39.2 wt%) and sulphur (S2p, 1.8 wt%) elements can be clearly seen in Fig. 3a. As shown in Fig. 3b, the C1s spectrum is convoluted into three peaks corresponding to CC/CC (284.5 eV), CN/CO (285.9 eV) and CO/CN (288.2 eV). The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) can be deconvoluted into the peak of CN (399.0 eV). The O1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) can be assigned as CO (531.0 eV) and CO (533.0 eV). The S 2p spectrum (Fig. 3e) corresponds to CS (164.1 eV). For ZrCDs, the XPS spectrum (Fig. 3f-k) analysis showed that the oxygen content of ZrCDs increased and the carbon and nitrogen content decreased (C 1 s 36.5 wt%, N 1 s 15.0 wt%, O 1 s 46.5 wt%, Zr 3d 11.3 wt% and S 2p 0.7 wt%). The O1s spectrum of ZrCDs (Fig. 3i) has three curves at 530.5 (Zr-O), 531.7 (CO) and 533.1 eV (CO). Two main peaks located at 182.5 and 184.9 eV in the Zr 3d XPS spectrum (Fig. 3k) are attributed to Zr 3d5/2 (Zr-O) and Zr 3d3/2 (Zr-O), respectively. The structure of CDs was further analyzed by FTIR spectrum [35–37]. As shown in Fig. S7a, the tensile vibration at 3440 cm−1 and 2950 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of OH and CH. The peaks at 1708 and 1630 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibration of CO (carboxylic acid) and CN, respectively. The above results prove that the FTIR data is basically consistent with the XPS analysis. It is worth noting that CDs and ZrCDs differ at 1635 cm−1 and 1398 cm−1 (Fig. S7a, red solid line), which may be associated with asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of zirconium carboxylate complexes, respectively [38,39]. The crystallinity of CDs was studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, as shown in Fig. S7b, a broad peak at 26° is attributed to the (0 0 2) crystal plane of graphite [40,41]. The XRD data of ZrCDs (Fig. S7c) are basically consistent with CDs, indicating that the doping of Zr does not affect the crystallinity of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of concentration-regulated luminescence of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Several control experiments were carried out to reveal the concentration-dependent luminescence mechanism of CDs. First, the morphologies and sizes of CDs were analyzed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 4 a, CD1 is in a monodisperse state with the average particle diameter of 5.3 nm. With the increase of concentration, the degrees of aggregation gradually enhanced, and the sizes of CD2 and CD3 were increased to 26.5 nm and 48.1 nm, respectively. For CD4, severe aggregation lead to that the size cannot be accurately quantified. At high concentrations, the uniform dispersion of CDs become unstable due to the extremely short distances between CDs and high surface energy, thus CDs tends to aggregate together in order to be stable in solution. By contrast, ZrCD1, ZrCD2, ZrCD3 and ZrCD4 have no obvious aggregation with monodispersity (Fig. 4b). After quantitative analysis, the average sizes of ZrCD1, ZrCD2, ZrCD3 and ZrCD4 are 10.1 nm, 13.6 nm, 14.7 nm and 16.0 nm, respectively. Thus, ZrCDs do not aggregate obviously with increasing concentration, and the size did not change significantly. Based on the above data, it can be inferred that concentration-dependent fluorescence of CDs may result from aggregation [31].

The absolute PL quantum yield (QY) of CD1 to CD6 is collected in Fig. 5 a. As the concentration increases, the QY value increases first and then decreases from CD2 (6.3%) to CD6 (1.9%). The asymmetric excitation-emission matrix of CDs (Fig. 1a) implies the existence of a self-absorption phenomenon. Inner filter effect [42,43], aggregation [44,45] and self-absorption [46,47] may coexist when the concentration increases, which leads to the self-quenching phenomenon and the decrease of QY. In contrast, with increasing the concentration of ZrCDs, QY increased first and then slightly reduced (Fig. 5b). In addition, Fig. 5c, d respectively show the fluorescence spectra of CD1-CD6 and ZrCD1-ZrCD6 at different excitation wavelengths during QY measurement. The zeta-potentials of CDs at different concentrations are shown in Fig. 6 a, since there are lots of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, the zeta-potential of CDs (0.002 mg mL−1) is −10.59 mV. With the concentration going up to 0.05 mg mL−1, the zeta-potential of CDs changed to 0.07 mV. Even though the concentration was increased from 0.05 to 1 mg mL−1, zeta-potential still stayed at 0 mV, these results implied that CDs were not stable and more likely to aggregate into large particles. The zeta potential of ZrCDs (0.002 mg mL−1) is −4.75 mV, which is more positive than that of CDs (0.002 mg mL−1), due to the formation of zirconium complexes on the surface of ZrCDs. Moreover, the zeta-potential of ZrCDs gradually decreased from −4.75 mV to −17.83 mV as the concentration increased, indicating that ZrCDs were in a highly dispersed state.

To further investigate the effects of aggregation on CDs luminescence, we analyzed the solid state fluorescence of CDs and ZrCDs. As shown in Fig. 6b, c, the fluorescence of CDs in solid state is weak, while that of ZrCDs is strong. The fluorescence images of solid CDs and ZrCDs under the excitation of 365 nm and 500 nm were displayed in Fig. 6d, e, no emission was observed for CDs. However, ZrCDs can emit blue and red colors under 365 nm and 500 nm irradiation, respectively. The absorption spectra of solid CDs and ZrCDs show (Fig. 6f) that CDs have a very broad absorption across UV–VIS-NIR with the strongest peaks at 238 nm and 339 nm, while the absorption band ZrCDs is weaker than that of CDs with two typical bands at 237 nm and 335 nm. The above data demonstrate that CDs remain excitation-dependent luminescence in aggregation state. On the other hand, the high absorbance of CDs in solid state causes a self-quenching effect of CDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching in the solid state [24,48].

The fluorescence decay curves of CDs are shown in Table S3 and Fig. S8a, which can be fitted by a diexponential function, suggesting that there existed multiple decay channels. The average lifetime of CDs increased from 3.44 ns to 6.45 ns at the concentration range of 0.002 mg mL−1 to 0.25 mg mL−1. And then the lifetime reduced to 4.47 ns (CD5, 0.5 mg mL−1) and 2.24 ns (CD6, 1.0 mg mL−1). Such differences indicate that CDs have multiple luminescence centers and various radiative transitions. Within a reasonable concentration range, the aggregation could reduce the surface defects, induce non-radiative cross relaxation between CDs and bring about the increase of decay time. However, at a very high concentration, the energy or electron transfers between nanoparticles would shorten the fluorescence lifetime dramatically [52–55]. In contrast, because zirconium complexes hampers the aggregation and intermolecular interactions of ZrCDs, the emission lifetime of ZrCDs increased slightly first and then generally maintain stability (Table S4 and Fig. S8b).

In summary, Carbon dots (CDs) with remarkable concentration-dependent luminescence were synthesized via a simple one-step pyrolysis method. By contrast, Zr-doped CDs (ZrCDs) with concentration-independent emission were prepared in a similar way. As the concentration increases, the aggregation of CDs results in a continuous fluorescence redshift. On the contrary, the existence of zirconium carboxylate impedes ZrCDs from aggregation and renders ZrCDs with a good dispersion, which helps ZrCDs be inert to concentration. Based on the results mentioned above, we can draw the conclusion that aggregation is the origin of concentration-modulated fluorescence, which induce multicolor emission of CDs. This work provides a new strategy for rationally modulating the optical performance of CDs and will open up a novel avenue for exploiting the potential applications of CDs.

Ya Su: Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing - original draft. Zhigang Xie: Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Min Zheng: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023 and 51522307), Talent Development Fund of Jilin Province and the Research Project of Science and Technology of the Education Department of Jilin Province (No. JJKH20200648KJ). The authors acknowledge the help of Prof. Siyu Lu.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.04.004.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 53% Efficient red emissive carbon quantum dots for high color rendering and stable warm white-light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, F. Yuan, X. Li, Y. Li, H. Zhong, L. Fan, et al., 53% Efficient red emissive carbon quantum dots for high color rendering and stable warm white-light-emitting diodes, Adv. Mater. 29 (2017) 1702910.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red fluorescent carbon dots with phenylboronic acid tags for quick detection of Fe(III) in PC12 cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Li, Y. Wang, X. Zhang, X. Guo, X. Kang, L. Du, Y. Liu, Red fluorescent carbon dots with phenylboronic acid tags for quick detection of Fe(III) in PC12 cells, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 526 (2018) 487-496.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared excitation/emission and multiphoton-induced fluorescence of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Li, P. Jing, L. Sun, Y. An, X. Shan, X. Lu, et al., Near-infrared excitation/emission and multiphoton-induced fluorescence of carbon dots, Adv. Mater. 30 (2018) 1705913.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots with excitation-independent emission for selective bioimaging and gene delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yang, Y. Wang, X. Shen, C. Su, J. Yang, M. Piao, F. Jia, G. Gao, L. Zhang, Q. Lin, One-step synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots with excitation-independent emission for selective bioimaging and gene delivery, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 492 (2017) 1-7.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In vivo theranostics with near-infrared-emitting carbon dots-highly efficient photothermal therapy based on passive targeting after intravenous administration

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Bao, Y. Yuan, J. Chen, B. Zhang, D. Li, D. Zhou, et al., In vivo theranostics with near-infrared-emitting carbon dots-highly efficient photothermal therapy based on passive targeting after intravenous administration, Light Sci. Appl. 7 (2018) 91.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots for in vivo bioimaging and theranostics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Du, N. Xu, J. Fan, W. Sun, X. Peng, Carbon dots for in vivo bioimaging and theranostics, Small 15 (2019) 1805087.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots with tunable negative charges for bio-imaging in bacterial viability assessment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Lu, Y. Song, H. Huang, Y. Liu, Y. Fu, J. Huang, et al., Fluorescent carbon dots with tunable negative charges for bio-imaging in bacterial viability assessment, Carbon 120 (2017) 95-102.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared light-mediated photodynamic/photothermal therapy nanoplatform by the assembly of Fe3O4 carbon dots with graphitic black phosphorus quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhang, W. Wang, Y. Cui, N. Zhou, J. Shen, Near-infrared light-mediated photodynamic/photothermal therapy nanoplatform by the assembly of Fe3O4 carbon dots with graphitic black phosphorus quantum dots, Int. J. Nanomedicine 13 (2018) 2803-2819.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile approach to synthesize highly fluorescent multicolor emissive carbon dots via surface functionalization for cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kundu, J. Lee, B. Park, C. Ray, K.V. Sankar, W.S. Kim, S.H. Lee, I.J. Cho, S.C. Jun, Facile approach to synthesize highly fluorescent multicolor emissive carbon dots via surface functionalization for cellular imaging, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 513 (2018) 505-514.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable photoluminescence across the entire visible spectrum from carbon dots excited by white light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Hu, A. Trinchi, P. Atkin, I. Cole, Tunable photoluminescence across the entire visible spectrum from carbon dots excited by white light, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. Engl. 54 (2015) 2970-2974.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-colour carbon dots: integration of multiple emission centres into single particles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Ju, H. Nie, Z. Liu, H. Xu, M. Li, C. Wu, et al., Full-colour carbon dots: integration of multiple emission centres into single particles, Nanoscale 9 (2017) 13326-13333.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbogenic nanodots derived from organo-templated zeolites with modulated full-color luminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Mu, N. Wang, Z. Sun, J. Wang, J. Li, J. Yu, Carbogenic nanodots derived from organo-templated zeolites with modulated full-color luminescence, Chem. Sci. 7 (2016) 3564-3568.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, S.B. Yu, J.S. Wei, H.M. Xiong, Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism, ACS Nano 10 (2016) 484-491.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A building brick principle to create transparent composite films with multicolor emission and self-healing function
